coredata.dc:title: Gram-scale synthesis of highly doped chlorine graphene quantum dots: Synthesis and photoluminescence properties
coredata.dc:creator: Rady, Hossam Salah
Misbah, M. Hamed
El-Kemary, Maged
coredata.dc:description: Band-gap engineering of graphene-based nanomaterials has a huge influence on their optical and structural properties. Our contribution reports the successful gram-scale synthesis of highly chlorinated graphene quantum dots (Cl-GQDs) via a two-step preparation method. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) proved the successful doping of chlorine atoms into the as-prepared graphene oxide quantum dots (O-GQDs). Time-resolved fluorescence revealed a high decrease in the relaxation lifetime from 690 ps for O-GQDs to 5.3–4 ps for Cl-GQDs. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) confirms that all nanoparticles are on the quantum scale (4–6 nm). The large blueshift in photoluminescence (PL) 150 nm from the original O-GQD (440 → 290 nm) supports the chlorination effect which enables tuning the band gap of O-GQD.

head.ce:title: Gram-scale synthesis of highly doped chlorine graphene quantum dots: Synthesis and photoluminescence properties

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Nanoscience & Nanotechnology, Kafrelsheikh University, Kafrelsheikh, 33511, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Nanoscience & Nanotechnology, Kafrelsheikh University, Kafrelsheikh, 33511, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Band-gap engineering of graphene-based nanomaterials has a huge influence on their optical and structural properties. Our contribution reports the successful gram-scale synthesis of highly chlorinated graphene quantum dots (Cl-GQDs) via a two-step preparation method. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) proved the successful doping of chlorine atoms into the as-prepared graphene oxide quantum dots (O-GQDs). Time-resolved fluorescence revealed a high decrease in the relaxation lifetime from 690 ps for O-GQDs to 5.3–4 ps for Cl-GQDs. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) confirms that all nanoparticles are on the quantum scale (4–6 nm). The large blueshift in photoluminescence (PL) 150 nm from the original O-GQD (440 → 290 nm) supports the chlorination effect which enables tuning the band gap of O-GQD.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon has always played a significant role in the advancement of modern science and technology as one of the fundamental components of the human body, the industrial world, and society. The carbon family gained three new members, carbon quantum dots, graphene, and fullerene, in addition to graphite, carbon nanotubes, fullerene and graphitic carbon nitride [1–6]. Since it was discovered a decade ago [7], graphene exhibits tremendous fields of applications because of its unique properties, opening the door for further theories and applications of 2D alternatives [8–13]. Going down inside graphene, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a zero-dimension (0D) allotrope of (2D) graphene in the range of 1–10 nm [8,14]. Unlike zero-band gap graphene, GQDs exhibit tunable absorption over a wide range of wavelengths -from UV to IR - according to particle size because of quantum confinement [15]. Such excitation causes an electronic transition between the highest occupied molecular orbitals (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO), and photoluminescence (PL) usually occurs in the reverse direction [16]. GQDs exhibit properties that are derived from both graphene and quantum dots (QDs), combining the structure of monolayered graphene with the edge effects, nonzero band gap, and quantum confinement effects of QDs. They possess unique optical, electrical and thermoelectric properties [17–22]. Therefore, they have wide applications in sensors [23], energy storage and conversion [24,25], biomedical applications [26–29], photodynamic therapy [30,31], photonics and optoelectronics [17,32,33].

The synthesis of GQDs has attracted many research groups to pursue crystalline and superior optical properties [34,35]. GQDs have the same synthetic approaches as nanomaterials: bottom up and top down. The bottom-up approach mainly involves controlled pyrolysis of organic molecules (e.g., glucose) in alkaline solution [36]. Controlled destruction of large-size carbon-based materials such as graphite oxide [37], multiwalled carbon nanotubes [38], expanded graphite [39] and carbon fibers [40].

Heteroatom doping is one of the most promising techniques to enhance optoelectronic properties by introducing foreign atoms to pure GQDs [31,41–43]. When compared to other elements, halogens are well recognized as extremely electronegative. When it links with carbon, it forms an electrophilic area that transforms halogen atoms into electron donors with unequal distributions of electron densities in covalent bonds [44]. The physical properties of the carbon-halogen bond (C-X) vary along the group; as the electronegativity decreases down the group (F > Cl > Br > I), the C-X donor ability increases in the opposite order (I > Br > Cl > F). Although the atomic radii steadily increase down the groups, the electron affinities do not follow the normal order (Cl > F > Br > I). For similar structures, the C-X bond strength as electron donors will follow the order of C-X sp > C-X sp 2 > C-X sp 3 [45]. These properties and C-X characteristics are prospective for improving halogenated GQD (X-GQD) materials in various fields.

This gives the chlorine atom a unique effect in carbon-based nanomaterials. Noor-Ul-Ain et al. used HCl as a source of chlorine doping as a selective probe detector with efficient detection for Pb+2, with a change in life time from 2.14 ns before doping to 2.39 nm after doping. However, such a property appears to be associated with co-doping by sulfur atoms [46–48]. More advanced photovoltaic photodetectors were carried out by Zhao et al. [49] by increasing the photocurrent of doped Cl-GQD P3HT by 105 times compared to the dark current at room temperature when irradiated with a 405 nm laser. While in their other publication [50], the Cl-GQDs enhanced the carrier concentration of the P3HT composite by 30% and decreased the depletion layer width in comparison with free Cl-GQD composites. They prepared the Cl-GQD from carbon fibers of degreased cotton at high temperatures. Another doping technique is the incorporation of Cl-doped carbon by thermal annealing of carbon black with polyvinyl chloride [51]. Although this method produces amorphous chlorine-doped carbon, it produces chlorinated carbon with high faraday efficiency, leading to enhanced N2 reduction. Focusing on white light-emitting diodes (LEDs), white light irradiation draws the attention of few researchers. The doping of carbon-based nanomaterials by chlorine atoms changes their electronic structure by introducing new levels. Li et al. [52] prepared Cl-GQD by hydrothermal reaction based on 1,5-diaminonaphthalene and trichloromethane with broad absorption spectra and dual emission spectra (fluorescence and phosphorescence) and a highly efficient white LED (WLED). Solid state WLEDs were further improved by incorporating Cl-GQDs with polymer by drop casting on a silicon resin matrix [53]. Impeding Cl-GQDs in the polymer reduced luminescence quenching with long color stability. On the contrary, Huang et al. found that irradiation of N/Cl co-doped GQD is a more developed technique in wound healing in comparison with chlorine free N-GQD [31]. Their results show better wound healing after irradiating polymeric co-doped GQDs with WLED for a minute, which proves that N/Cl co-doped GQDs have promising photodynamic inactivation of bacteria. Electrochemical exfoliation is an efficient technique which was used to produce high quality Cl-GQD. The prepared Cl-GQD exhibits highly capacitive and energy storage performance. However, the decrease in electronic conductivity with increasing Cl content was not studied with optical properties and the doping effect on the electronic levels of the QDs [54].

In this work, we reported a large-scale synthesis of GQDs via two-step preparation routes including ultrasonic treatment in dimethyl formamide (DMF) followed by solvothermal preparation. The ultrasonic step was used for extra exfoliation and size reduction of graphene oxide (GO). That is, by weakening any existing interlayer van der Waals forces and oxygen/hydrogen bonds through forced intercalation of solvent into GO layers. However, solvothermal cutting of the sonicated GO solution will produce GQDs [55,56]. Our solvothermal step of the chlorination process involves adding chlorine sources, which should facilitate the reaction with efficient synthesis free of impurities [57]. A detailed study of the effect of dopant (chlorine atom) concentration on the optical and structural properties of chlorinated graphene quantum dots (Cl-GQDs) is also provided. Additionally, the relaxation lifetime was successfully reduced from 690 ps in O-GQDs to 5.3, 5.0 and 4.0 ps in Cl-GQDs, with an approximate blueshift in PL equal to 150 nm.

Graphite 200 mesh, (99.9995%, AlfaAeser). Sulfuric acid (98%), phosphoric acid (85%), nitric acid (69%), potassium permanganate (KMnO4), 50% H2O2, dimethyl formamide (DMF), thionyl chloride (SOCl2, 99%), and acetonitrile all from MERCK were used as received without further treatment.

We prepared GO using an improved Hummer's method with slight modifications [58]. In an ice bath, 5 g of graphite was added to a mixture of concentrated acids under vigorous stirring (sulfuric acid: phosphoric acid: nitric acid) with a volumetric ratio of 30:3:1. After 2 h of mixing, we added 30 g KMnO4 stepwise to the above mixture. We left the reaction to run at the same stirring speed for 6 days at 70 °C to improve oxidative exfoliation. Consequently, 2 L of deionized water was added to the slurry, followed by dropping 5 ml H2O2 until clear formation of a caramel-colored precipitate. The supernatant was decanted, and the precipitate was collected, washed with Milli-Q water and centrifuged at 12500 r.p.m for 20 min. The washing step was repeated a total of five times. Hereafter, the precipitate was washed twice with 1% HCl solution to remove metal chelation on the surface of GO sheets before collecting and drying in a freeze dryer to obtain GO sheets. Using a thin film applicator, the dried butter-like paste was screen printed 500 nm thick on a glass substrate (2 × 2 cm2) for XPS and XRD analysis. The final yield was 7.8 g.
To ensure better cutting and doping of the GO sheets, we prepared O-GQDs with two-step preparation pathways: ultrasonication in organic solvent and the solvothermal method (Scheme 1 ) [33,59,60].

First, 10 g of the dried GO was sonicated in a solution of 450 ml DMF mixed with 150 ml H2O2 for 6 h (operation cycle was 2 min on, 1 min off) in ice bath. Second, 170 ml of the homogenized solution (containing 2.8 g GO) was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave (250 ml), placed in an oven at 200 °C for 12 h and left to cool. Then, we separated the graphitic precipitates by decantation followed by centrifugation at 15000 r.p.m. for 30 min and finally filtered through a 0.22 μm PVDF membrane. We used an oil bath rotary evaporator (RotaVap) to remove DMF after mixing the solution with acetonitrile (6:1 v/v). Hereafter, the precipitate was dissolved in 50 ml Milli-Q water and dialyzed in a 1 kDa MWCO sack for 3 days. Finally, the sample was freeze-dried for 24 h to obtain ≈2.463 g solid O-GQD powder.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of different doping concentrations of Cl-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To the homogenized solution, we added different volumes of SOCl2 to achieve different doping ratios. Samples were named (Cl-GDQ 1, Cl-GDQ 2, Cl-GDQ 3) for SOCl2 volumes (10, 20, and 30 ml) then transferred to Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave (250 ml) and placed in an oven at 200 °C for 12 h. Separation and purification were performed as mentioned above with O-GQDs. Moreover, it is important to mention that SO2 gas was expected to evolve during the removal of DMF in the RotaVap step. Therefore, we received the greenish-brown gas in aqueous sodium hydroxide solution (3 M) for further safety. The observed final yields were 3.4 g (44.4%), 8.6 g (68.7%), and 9.5 g (54.7%) for Cl-GDQ 1, Cl-GDQ 2, and Cl-GDQ 3 respectively.

JASCO FTIR-6800 was used to obtain Fourier transform infrared spectra. A JASCO spectrophotometer V-770 was used to study UV–visible absorption (UV–Vis) spectra in transmission mode, and photoluminescence (PL) was obtained by a JASCO spectrofluorometer FP-8600. The relaxation lifetime for the aqueous solution of all QDs was measured using the time-correlated single photon counting technique by a FluoTime 200 time-resolved spectrometer with a picosecond laser (λ = 375 nm) for excitation as described elsewhere [61]. We confirmed the quantum size, shape and thickness of the prepared GQDs and d-spacing of the QD planes by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) (JEOL-JEM-2100F) and atomic force microscope (AFM) WITec Alpha 300 A which was operated in tapping. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were collected using a Bruker D2 Advance with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy was performed by a Thermo-Scientific K-Alpha XPS system with an Al Ka micro-focused monochromator X-ray source, which precisely and accurately measured the dopant ratios. All samples were spin coated on glass slides (1 cm2) to form a thickness of ≈10 nm for XPS analysis. We used CasaXPS software for high-resolution fitting (H.R.F of peaks with multiple components and elemental percentage analysis using a standard reference table of relative sensitivity factors (R.S.F.) in CasaXPS software after subtracting the Shirly background [62,63].

Previous approaches for producing GQDs usually use a low amount of graphene oxide (GO) precursor at either high temperature for a short duration or vice versa [59,60]. Herein, we produced GQDs in gram-scale through solvothermal process at 200 °C, which is considered a moderate-to-high temperature. This temperature enables the conversion of a significant portion of a highly concentrated GO suspension into GQDs over a 12-h period [64,65].

We suggest two main mechanism pathways of nucleophilic substitution reaction of Cl− ions which will replace the electrophilic oxygen groups (-OH and –COOH) on the O-GQD. First, the carboxylic pathway: upon the addition of the strong Lewis acid; SOCl2, the carbonyl group of the nucleophilic –COOH group is bonding with electrophilic sulfur atom by removing the carboxylic hydrogen forming chlorosulfite ester and Cl− ion. Secondly, the free Cl− ions attacks the carbonyl group in the formed ester which destabilize the intermediate molecule leading to the release of SO2 gas and HCl (Scheme 2 A) [66]. Second, the hydroxyl pathway: where the lone pair of electrons on oxygen atoms attacks thionyl sulfur forming protonated chlorosulfite and releasing Cl− ion which attacks the hydroxyl α-carbon atom and releasing the SO2 gas and HCl (Scheme 2 B) [67,68].

We used XPS to determine the atomic percent (at%) of chlorine atoms successfully doped during the solvothermal cutting of GO. Fig. 1 displays the analysis of the XPS spectrum for all GQDs. The observed binding energy of chlorine dopants is at 200 eV, while the binding energies for the GO sample are 286 eV and 533 eV for C1s and O1s, respectively.

Calculations of the atomic ratios of C:O:Cl were carried out by CasaXPS software, which confirms the assumption of increasing the doping ratio with increasing doping precursor (SOCl2), as shown in Table 1 .

Also, it's shown in Fig. 1, the observed clear reduction in O1s for GO when compared with O1s of O-GQD is due to the solvothermal process [69]. In Fig. 2 B, H.R.F. of O-GQDs; C1s is assigned to the main functional groups (CC/C–C 283.4 eV, C–OH 284.5 eV, CO 285.6 eV and O–CO 286.8 eV), whereas O1s is assigned to (O–CO 531.3 eV, C–OH 532.1 eV and CO 533.1 eV). Cl-GQDs have two peaks at 200.16 eV and 220.65 eV corresponding to Cl2p3/2 Cl2p1/2, as summarized in Table 2 . The intensity of the Cl2p peaks clearly increased with increasing atomic percent (at %) of dopant in the order Cl-GQD 1 < Cl-GQD 2 < Cl-GQD 3 (see Table 1). Such enhancement confirms the change in optical and structural properties.

In Fig. 2 C-E. The chlorination process slightly shifts C1s fitted values to higher binding energies because of doping with highly electronegative chlorine atoms. However, the O1s fitted values are not noticeably shifted for Cl-GQDs. The previous outcomes support the assumption that most chlorine dopants were bonded with carbon atoms more than oxygen functional groups. Furthermore, the extra formation/narrowing of a clear C–C peak after doping proves the formation of new carbon sp3 bonds.

XRD patterns of QDs show the phase transformation of crystal systems with doping and quantization. First, O-GQD shows broad peaks at 2θ = 24.7641° and 31.3976° corresponding to hexagonal graphitic (002) and (100) planes [70]. and orthorhombic plane (121) at 2θ = 18.499° (Ref: 96-151-3556). This proves the multi-crystalline nature of the prepared GQD [71]. The broadening in (002) is due to a chemical cutting of GO to smaller QDs with fewer functional oxygen groups on edges, which enhanced the interlayer spacing of O-GQDs [40] with disordered stacking of graphene layers and disappearance of the XRD peak of GO at 10.4° [72] Fig. 3 (inset).

Cl-GQDs exhibit sharp XRD peaks at about 218° (Reference: 96-151-3556), hexagonal peaks at roughly 2 = 24.76° and 31.4°, and monoclinic peaks at around 2 = 32.65° (Reference: 96-402-1653). The last monoclinic peak represents the presence of chlorine atoms as the peak intensity is obviously increased with Cl atom content. Furthermore, the sharpness of Cl-GQDs peaks arises from the increase in d-spacing as a result of chlorination process resulting in improvement in the overall crystallinity of all Cl-GQDs with a slight shift towards lower angles was observed with an increase in the chlorine content.

To examine the successful quantization of GO sheets, we performed HR-TEM (Fig. 4 (1–5)), selected area electron diffraction (Fig. 4(i–v)) and normal distribution histograms (Fig. 4(a–d)) by dipping carbon microgrids (Ted Pella Inc., 400 Mesh Nickel Grid) in GQD solutions. Figs. 4–1 exhibits few wrinkled layers of GO, as shown in the inset histogram (10 nm scale bar). Fig. 4 (3–5) exhibits the HR-TEM of spherical and crystalline Cl-GQDs with a uniform quantum size distribution for all samples. O-GQD Figs. 4–2 has a d-spacing of 0.248 nm, which is slightly below the fundamental interplanar distance of the hexagonal graphene lattice (0.246 nm) [73,74]. Such findings can be attributed to the presence of terminal oxygen functional groups that stretch lattice fringes, giving such an increase in d-spacing [75]. The hexagonally distributed pattern of SAED (Fig. 4-i) exhibits singly crystalline O-GQDs because of the hexagonal-structured graphene core of the QDs [76]. On the other hand, SEAD of chlorinated GQD; Fig. 4 (iii-v), exhibits a multi crystalline phase, which is in accordance with the aforementioned XRD results.

The average size and the small increase in d-spacing values that resulted from doping are summarized in Table 3 . The electron affinity and rising chlorine dopant concentration could be the reason for this increase. Additionally, the enhanced interplanar spacing confirms that the proposed synthesis route was successful in chlorine doping inside the graphitic core by halogen addition to carbon‒carbon bonds (sp2 to sp3), which was confirmed earlier by XPS [50].

The prepared samples were spin coated on mica substrates and air dried for AFM measurements Fig. 5 . Lateral heights of the GQD particles were calculated by Project Five software in air at room temperature. Fig. 5(a–d) shows the tapping mode AFM images with topographic highest (insets) of O-GQD, Cl-GQD1, Cl-GQD2 and Cl-GQD3 on mica substrate, respectively. Fig. 5 depicts nanostructures with size of 15–45 nm and topographic height of ≈1–3 nm, revealing that GQDs are consisted of 2 ± 1 graphene layers [53,77]. The nanoclusters detected by the AFM are thought to be made of more than a few GQDs, as shown in TEM Fig. 4 (2–5). As a result, AFM and TEM are complementing methods for determining the height, size, and form of GQDs.

As shown in Fig. 6 , we followed the change in the functional groups during the preparation process. GO shows identical carbon and oxygen bonds (3520, 1805, 1660, 1577 and 1175 cm-1) corresponding to symmetrical stretching vibrations of –OH, CO, alkene CC, aromatic CC, and C–O, respectively. As a result of size reduction in the form of quantum dot O-GQDs, there was a significant increase in the intensities of the stretching vibration of etheric C–OH at 1130 cm−1 and the bending vibration of the phenolic –OH group at 1384 cm−1. Furthermore, C–Cl vibrational bands appear at approximately 610, 865 and 1025 cm-1, providing evidence of successful chlorination [72].

When this low electron affinity halogen is bonded with the graphitic structures, the symmetric stretching vibrations of O-GQD at 2765 and 2435 cm−1 of sp2 C–H and aldehydic –COH, respectively, are improved [50,75]. However, the intensities of the stretching vibration of (-OH) at 3440 cm−1 were seldom changed in all samples. As mentioned in the XPS results, doping GQDs reduces oxygen due to solvothermal cutting to a smaller size. Moreover, the reduction potential of dopant precursors removes oxygen functional groups and converts sp2-hybridized orbitals to a more sp3 matrix, leading to more extra vibration modes [75,78]. The bending (-OH) absorption at 1384 cm−1 was reduced with enhancement of the bending alkane peak of (C–H) at 1470 cm−1, indicating that the substitution reactions of the dopants replaced bending hydroxyl and alkene groups to form C–Cl and C–H [79,80].

The effect of chlorine doping was further studied using UV–Vis and PL spectroscopy. Fig. 7 illustrates UV–visible absorption spectra of aqueous solutions of QDs before and after the chlorination process. The absorption spectra of O-GQDs consist of a strong band at 204 nm and two weak bands at 240 nm and 300 nm. The broad band at the tail of O-GQD ≈300 nm, which is attributed to carbon‒oxygen-based functional groups, is a result of the (

Such peaks were blue-shifted upon doping compared to O-GQD. This shift is due to the reconstruction of the bandgap energy and the transition between the HOMO-LUMO electron transitions by introducing the electronic levels of halogens. After chlorination, it was noticeable that the peak at 193 nm was repeated in all Cl-GQDs, which represents the (
σ →
π *
)
transition of aromatic/carbene CC [49]. Such transitions were influenced by the new energy levels of chlorine that had been introduced in the electronic structure of GQD, leading to a widened band gap and pushing the HOMO level to lower values (blue shift) [79].

Since the GQDs are composed mainly of a CC core, all substituents/dopants are located on the surface of the QDs [84]. As mentioned above, the transitions of the sp3 matrix (
σ →

)
affect the overall transitions to lower wavelengths due to quantum confinement for sp2 cluster (

)
transitions. This is obvious in redshifted beaks 306, 262 and 210 nm: (n→π*) transitions with increasing chlorine at% in Cl-GQD [84].

The optical band gap of GQD is determined by the size, shape, and chemical composition of the GQD. In our work, the band gap is defined by the difference between HOMO and LUMO molecular orbitals. As electron withdrawing group, Cl dopant alters the optical band gap of GQD through lowering LUMO levels by inserting new electronic levels above Fermi level leading to changing the electronic structure of the GQD leading to extra lowering of the band gap with increasing the Cl-content, see Table 4 [77,85,86].

From Fig. 8 , we calculated the optical band gap of the as prepared GQDs by Tauc equation (1):

GQDs have the same properties as other semiconductor quantum dots with few differences. One of them is that the emission depends not only on the size but also on the carbene structure of zigzag edges [87] and substituents such as oxygen, nitrogen, and halides [23,88–90]. Moreover, the high optical heterogeneity of each particle is not precisely defined [91]. Fig. 9 exhibits the PL spectra of the as-prepared GQDs under one excitation wavelength (λEx) of 225 nm and the same concentration (0.5 mg/ml) for all aqueous samples. This excitation wavelength enables deeper detection of transitions and accurately measure the corresponding quantum yields. This resulted in a reduced number of excited chromophores that typically absorb at higher wavelengths. Consequently, the influence of surface defects on the outcomes of this study was effectively eliminated when compared with other candidates listed in Table 7 [47,48,92].

The maximum emission wavelengths (λ max) of the Cl-GQDs (λPL) show a blueshift (126 nm) when compared with those of the O-GQDs (416 nm–290 nm), with an enhanced quantum yield (QY) from 3% in the case of the O-GQDs to approximately 16% in the case of the Cl-GQDs with increasing the hetero-atom dopant at%, as published elsewhere [52] (Table 5 ). QY was measured against aqueous solution of quinine sulfate (qs) in 0.1 M H2SO4 as a reference where the calculations follow Equation (2) [93].

Where I and Iqs are the measured integrated emission intensities of GQD samples and qs solution, η and ηqs are refractive indexes of the solvent (in this case water = 1.333) and finally A and Aqs are the optical densities of GQD samples and qs. Such enhancement of quantum yield can be attributed to the fact that doping with electronegative atom chlorine; increases the number of available energy levels, which increases the probability of radiative recombination and thus increases the quantum yield [94,95].

Higher λPL peaks (290 nm) arise from the relaxation transition from the carbon sp2 core to the surface functionalized groups [96,97]. Also, from Fig. 9, it is apparent that as the at% of chlorine dopant increased, λ max decreased in the order of Cl-GQD 1 < Cl-GQD 2 < Cl-GQD 3 upon excitation at 225 nm and aqueous Cl-GQD concentrations. This is related to the halogenation of GQDs with new surface states of Cl-GQDs and lowering the band gap as discussed above, where the absorption bands (n→π*) are blue shifted near λEx [98]. This explains the peak tail at 370 nm which represents the relaxation fluorescence of the (n→π*).
Where; τ is the relaxation life time in ps or ns.
a 1 and a 2 are exponential contributions which refer to the decay of fluorescence intensity over time.
χ2
the Fitting residual which is a measure of the difference between observed data and model fit.

We reported studies of the effective large-scale production of highly chlorinated graphene quantum dots to elucidate a proper/potential optoelectronic material. All prepared GQDs are on the quantum scale <7 nm with uniform size and shape distributions and graphene-thickness nature. XPS results suggested successful chlorination with different atomic ratios and direct C–Cl bond formation. The aqueous dispersions of all prepared GQDs were stable and did not exhibit any sedimentation. Noticeable PL blueshift (126 nm) for Cl-GQDs in comparison with the original O-GQD revealing the insertion of new energy levels between HOMO and LUMO and tuning band gap. Furthermore, XRD reveals the existence of a highly crystalline structure of three phases, which emphasizes in-plane carbon core expansion in the TEM d-spacing results. The electron-transfer and recombination rates reported here correlate well with the spatial stacking structures in the binding sites and with the function.

We wish to confirm that there are no known conflicts of interest associated with this publication and there has been no significant financial support for this work that could have influenced its outcome.

Hossam Salah Rady: Investigation, Formal analysis, of the, Data curation, and, Writing – original draft. M. Hamed Misbah: Supervision, and revised the manuscript. Maged El-Kemary: Supervision, Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Writing – review & editing, Project administration, and, Funding acquisition, All the authors approved the manuscript.
